title: History - Unit 13 - Modern - Governance, Administration, Education, Press - PYQ Toolkit - Forum IAS

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Who among the following was the first non-official member to be elected as Speaker of the Central Legislative Assembly on 24-81925?
questions.questionText: Who among the following was the first non-official member to be elected as Speaker of the Central Legislative Assembly on 24-81925?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Vithalbhai Patel** was a Gujarati lawyer and politician who was one of the most prominent leaders of the Indian independence movement.
He was elected as the first non-official member to be Speaker of the Central Legislative Assembly on 24 August **1925**. He was also a member of the Indian National Congress, and he served as the President of the Congress in **1930** and **1931**.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: The British Committee of the Indian National Congress was founded in
questions.questionText: The British Committee of the Indian National Congress was founded in
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The British Committee of the Indian National Congress was established in 1889 in Britain by the Indian National Congress.
Its primary objective was to bring attention to Indian issues among the British public, as the British government was responsible for governing India.
Major leaders involved in the committee included WC Banerjee, Dadabhai Naroji, and Charles Bradlaugh. The first chairman of the committee was William Wedderburn, and the secretary was William Digby.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Why did the early nationalists oppose the Council Act of 1892?
questions.questionText: Why did the early nationalists oppose the Council Act of 1892?

options.a: The number of members of the Imperial Legislative Council was increased.
options.b: The number of members of Provincial Councils was increased.
options.c: The Councils were given the right to discuss the annual budgets.
options.d: The Art did not give the Indians control over the public funds.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The early nationalists opposed the Council Act of 1892 because: The act did not give the Indians control over the public funds.
The members could only ask questions on the budget, but not vote on it or propose amendments to it. The act did not give adequate representation to the Indians.
The official majority was maintained in both central and provincial councils, and the non-official members were mostly nominated by the governor-general or governors.
The principle of indirect election was also unsatisfactory, as it excluded a large section of the Indian population from participating in the electoral process.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Examine these two statements carefully and
select the correct answer using the codes given below.
Statement I The Permanent Settlement was rarely extended to any region beyond Bengal.
Statement II After 1810, the agricultural prices declined affecting adversely the income of the Bengal Zamindars.
questions.questionText: Examine these two statements carefully and select the correct answer using the codes given below. Statement I The Permanent Settlement was rarely extended to any region beyond Bengal. Statement II After 1810, the agricultural prices declined affecting adversely the income of the Bengal Zamindars. Codes:

options.a: Both the statements are individually true and Statements II is the correct explanation of Statement I
options.b: Both the statements are individually true but Statement II is not the correct explanation of Statement I
options.c: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
options.d: Statement I is false but Statement II is true

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement I is correct: The Permanent Settlement was rarely extended to any region beyond Bengal.** The Permanent Settlement was an agreement between the East India Company and the Bengali landlords or zamindars to fix the land revenue in 1793. It was introduced by Lord Cornwallis in Bengal, Bihar and Odisha.
It granted the zamindars the hereditary rights of ownership of land and made them responsible for paying a fixed amount of revenue to the Company.
Statement II is not correct: After 1810, agricultural prices rose, increasing the value of harvest produce, and enlarging the income of the Bengal zamindars.
Since the revenue demand was fixed under the Permanent Settlement, the colonial state could not claim any share of this enhanced income. The zamindars benefited from this situation and accumulated wealth and power. They also invested in trade, commerce, banking and urban development.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which one of the following statements about the English Education Act of 1835 is not correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements about the English Education Act of 1835 is not correct?

options.a: It was proposed by Governor General William Bentinck on the advice of Macaulay.
options.b: It made English the language of instruction in Indian education system
options.c: With the formal institutionalization of English as the language of instruction, the stage was set for a new direction of Indian education.
options.d: To the existing Oriental Institutions fresh awards of stipends to students and the publication of classical texts were to continu

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. English Education Act of 1835 was based on the recommendations of Thomas Babington Macaulay, who argued that Western learning was superior and that English should be the medium of instruction.
The Act stated that the existing Oriental Institutions would receive no further support from the Company. Thus, there was no provision of fresh awards of stipends to students of existing Oriental Institutions.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Which one of the following statements about the colonial economy is not correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements about the colonial economy is not correct?

options.a: The British presence inhibited indigenous capitalism.
options.c: The 'white collective monopoly' came earliest and remained most pronounced in Eastern India.
options.d: The Bombay hinterland was difficult to penetrate before the construction of railways

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The colonial economy had the following effects on the Indian economy:

Option a is correct: The British presence inhibited indigenous capitalism by imposing monopolies, tariffs, and restrictions on Indian industries and trade.
Option b is not correct: Laissez-faire did not actively promote indigenous capitalism, but rather served the interests of British capitalists and traders at the expense of Indian producers and consumers.
Option c is correct: The 'white collective monopoly' came earliest and remained most pronounced in Eastern India, where the British East India Company established its first foothold and exploited the resources and markets of Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa.
Option d is correct: The Bombay hinterland was difficult to penetrate before the construction of railways, because of the rugged terrain, the resistance of local rulers, and the competition from other European powers

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Which one of the following has created the office of the Secretary of State for India?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following has created the office of the Secretary of State for India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The office of the Secretary of State for India was created by The Government of India Act 1858.
- This act transferred the power and territories of the British East India Company to the British Crown after the Indian Rebellion of 1857.
- The Secretary of State for India was the British Cabinet minister and the political head of the India Office responsible for the governance of British India, Aden, and Burma.
- He was assisted by a council of 15 members and reported to the Parliament of India.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Which one of the following statements about land tenure system is not correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements about land tenure system is not correct?

options.a: During the British rule in India three categories of land tenure system, viz. Zamindari, Mahalwari and Ryotwari were introduced.
options.b: Under Zamindari system, land was held by one person or at the most by a few joint owners who were responsible for the payment of land revenue.
options.c: Under the Mahalwari system, the agricultural lands belonged to the government..
options.d: Under Ryotwari system, the individual holders had the permanent rights over land and were directly responsible for payment of land revenu

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Under the Mahalwari system, the agricultural lands did not belong to the government, but rather to a group of village landlords or village communities.
The Mahalwari system was introduced in 1833. It was prevalent in Central Province, North-West Frontier, Agra, Punjab and Gangetic Valley. The mahals or estates were assessed as a whole and the revenue was distributed among the landlords or villagers according to their shares.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which journal was not associated with revolutionary activities?
questions.questionText: Which journal was not associated with revolutionary activities?

options.e: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The Young India, a weekly journal, was published by Mahatma Gandhi from **1919** to **1931**, and used by him to spread his ideology of non-violence and resistance against the British.
Hence, it was not associated with revolutionary activities. Whereas Sandhya, Yugantar and Gadar were associated with promoting the revolutionary activities.

questions.subject: History

statementLines: The Montagu-Chelmsford Report formed the basis of:
questions.questionText: The Montagu-Chelmsford Report formed the basis of:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The Montagu-Chelmsford Report formed the basis of the Government of India Act, **1919**. This Act is known as Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms.
Montagu was the Secretary of State for India and Lord Chelmsford was the Viceroy of India. The Government of India Act of **1919** came into force in **1921**.

questions.subject: History
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Amrit Bazar Patrika was founded by Sisir Kumar Ghosh and Motilal Ghosh. It was started on 20th February, 1868 in the village of Amrita Bazar as a Bengali weekly newspaper.
Sisir Kumar Ghosh had declared that the primary objective of his paper was "to awaken the people and infuse in them the noble spirit of patriotism".
Bal Gangadhar Tilak had acknowledged Sisir Kumar Ghosh as his Guru, and in his newspaper Kesari, he attempted to follow him.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which of the following papers was essentially the mouthpiece of the policies of liberals?
questions.questionText: Which of the following papers was essentially the mouthpiece of the policies of liberals?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The Leader was seen as a liberal party newspaper but it was not merely a mouthpiece organ. The newspaper criticized the British Raj, but also Congress leaders and even liberal colleagues. For instance, Gopal Krishna Gokhale, a liberal, was not spared for his support to the Press Act of **1910**. The Leader was one of the most influential English-language newspapers in India during British Raj.
It was founded by Madan Mohan Malviya, the paper was published in Allahabad. Under C. Y. Chintamani, a dynamic editor from **1909** to **1934**, it acquired a large readership in North India.
**

Important Tips Other Newspapers:
- New India: It was started by Annie Besant in **1914** as daily newspaper to highlight issues related to the Indian freedom struggle.
- Young India: Lala Lajpat Rai published the Young India journal in **1916**. It was started as a weekly magazine in English but the journal was immediately banned from circulation in India and Britain by the Colonial Government on the grounds of 'seditious' content.
The magazine was later published by Gandhi from **1919** to **1931** to spread his message of swaraj and non-violence.
- Free Press Journal: It is an Indian English-language daily newspaper that was established in **1928** by Swaminathan Sadanand.
It was a supporter of the Independence movement. It is the first Indian-owned English daily newspaper published from Mumbai.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who in America started to issue newspaper 'Free India'?
questions.questionText: Who in America started to issue newspaper 'Free India'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Tarak Nath Das started a monthly magazine in **1908** called "Free Hindusthan" to condemn policies of the British Indian government and encourage resistance.
He was a professor of political science at Columbia University and a visiting faculty in several other universities.
